name: Palma Old Town Trail
name_original: City tour por Palma de Mallorca
description: Explore Palma de Mallorca's rich history and stunning architecture on this easy 3.6-mile city hike, featuring iconic landmarks and coastal v
region_intro: Hiking around Palma offers diverse landscapes, from coastal paths to mountain ranges. The region is characterized by the rugged peaks of the Serra de Tramuntana, extensive pine forests, and numerous coves along the coastline. These natural features provide varied terrain for outdoor activities, catering to different levels of hiking experience. Best hiking trails around Palma The most popular hiking route is Palma Old Town Trail, a 3.6 miles (5.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 32 minutes to complete. This easy route explores the historic core of the city. Another top favourite among local hikers is Bellver Castle and Forest Loop, an easy 3.2 miles (5.2 km) path. This route winds through a pine forest and leads to the historic Bellver Castle, offering city views. Local hikers also love the Castillo de Cabrera and Punta de n'Ensiola lighthouse — circular route through the Cabrera Archipelago National Park, a 9.4 miles (15.2 km) trail leading through a protected marine-terrestrial park, often completed in about 4 hours 20 minutes. Hiking around Palma is defined by coastal paths, pine forests, and the rugged terrain of the Serra de Tramuntana mountains. The extensive network of routes offers options for various ability levels, from easy strolls to moderate mountain treks. The routes in Palma are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 2,700 reviews. More than 29,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Palma's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of hiking trails can I expect around Palma?
A: Palma offers a diverse range of hiking experiences. You'll find everything from gentle coastal paths with stunning sea views to challenging mountain treks in the &lt;strong&gt;Serra de Tramuntana&lt;/strong&gt;. The region also features routes through extensive pine forests, such as the &lt;em&gt;Bosc de Bellver&lt;/em&gt;, and trails leading to hidden coves and natural parks.

Q: How many hiking routes are available in the Palma area?
A: There are over 370 hiking routes available around Palma, catering to various skill levels. This includes a significant number of easy trails, moderate paths, and some more challenging options for experienced hikers.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Palma?
A: The best times for hiking in Palma are generally late summer/autumn or spring. During these periods, temperatures are cooler and more pleasant for outdoor activities. In spring, the landscapes are vibrant with flowers, while autumn still offers warm enough weather for a refreshing swim after your hike.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or casual walkers?
A: Yes, Palma has many easy trails. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999751292/bellver-castle-and-forest-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bellver Castle and Forest Loop&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 5.2 km path that winds through a pine forest and leads to the historic Bellver Castle, offering city views. Another easy route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33807904" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaça Major (Main Square), Palma de Mallorca – Fountain at Plaça de la Reina loop from Plaça d'Espanya&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 6 km long.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes near Palma?
A: Yes, many routes in Palma are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999751292/bellver-castle-and-forest-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bellver Castle and Forest Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular option. Further afield, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1000123096/castillo-de-cabrera-and-punta-de-n-ensiola-lighthouse-circular-route-through-the-cabrera-archipelago-national-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Cabrera and Punta de n'Ensiola lighthouse — circular route through the Cabrera Archipelago National Park&lt;/a&gt; offers a longer, moderate circular experience through a protected marine-terrestrial park.

Q: What kind of natural features or landmarks can I see while hiking around Palma?
A: Hiking around Palma offers access to stunning natural features and historical landmarks. You can explore the dramatic peaks of the &lt;strong&gt;Serra de Tramuntana&lt;/strong&gt;, discover hidden coves along the coastline, or wander through ancient olive groves. Notable landmarks include the 14th-century circular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/687473" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bellver Castle&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/79390" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Cura on Puig de Randa&lt;/a&gt;, which offers panoramic views.

Q: Are there any hiking trails with good viewpoints or cafes along the way?
A: Many trails in Palma offer fantastic viewpoints. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33791833" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Paseo Marítimo de Palma – Steps to Bellver Castle loop&lt;/a&gt; provides excellent city and sea views. The &lt;em&gt;Puig de Maria&lt;/em&gt; near Pollença also offers magnificent panoramic views over the Formentor peninsula and the Bay of Pollença, culminating at a medieval monastery which often has facilities.

Q: Are there any shaded hiking routes for warmer days?
A: Yes, for warmer days, consider routes that pass through the region's extensive pine and holm oak forests. The &lt;em&gt;Bosc de Bellver&lt;/em&gt;, for example, provides shaded paths, as does the path to &lt;em&gt;Ermita de la Trinitat&lt;/em&gt; near Valldemossa, which winds through a holm oak forest offering welcome shade.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Palma?
A: The hiking routes in Palma are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an impressive average rating of 4.4 stars from over 2,700 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, from coastal beauty to mountain vistas, and the well-maintained paths that cater to all abilities.

Q: Can I access hiking trails around Palma using public transport?
A: Yes, many trailheads around Palma are accessible via public transport. The regional government has invested in developing and marking trails, making them more accessible. For routes further afield, local bus services often connect Palma with towns and villages in the &lt;strong&gt;Serra de Tramuntana&lt;/strong&gt;, providing access to various starting points.

Q: Are there any specific permits required for hiking in certain areas, like natural parks?
A: For some protected areas, such as the &lt;strong&gt;Cabrera Natural Park&lt;/strong&gt;, access needs to be arranged in advance, which may involve permits or booking boat trips. While most general hiking trails do not require specific permits, it's always advisable to check the regulations for any natural parks or reserves you plan to visit.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers near Palma?
A: For experienced trekkers, the &lt;strong&gt;Serra de Tramuntana&lt;/strong&gt; mountain range offers challenging routes, including five summits over 1,000 meters. The famous &lt;em&gt;GR 221 Dry Stone Walk&lt;/em&gt;, while a long-distance trail, has sections that can be tackled as day hikes, offering rugged terrain and significant elevation changes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/95163" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Santuari de Cura via Puig de Randa&lt;/a&gt; is another challenging option.
tips: On the beautiful, rectangular Plaça Major, the main square in the Upper Town, which is seamlessly framed by yellow-painted facades and arcades, tourists are almost among themselves.

You don't really know how old this gate is. In all likelihood, it dates from the 5th century and is of Roman origin. However, it was also part of the Moorish city fortifications, hence the current name "Arch of the Citadel". However, the arch itself and the rooms above it only date from the early Middle Ages, when the Aragons already ruled over Palma.
The "Arab Baths" are one of the few relics of Moorish architecture in the old town of Palma. The inner garden is an oasis of calm in the middle of the lively old town.

The first city wall was built by the Romans, probably around 400. This was almost completely destroyed by the Vandals around 430 AD. Some 500 years later, the Moors built a new one on the remains of the Roman wall, which was expanded in the 11th century.
When pirate attacks increased in the Middle Ages, it was decided in 1575 to strengthen and expand the existing, almost 700 year old walls. It took 226 years to complete. Completed in 1801, demolition began again in 1902. It lasted almost 30 years and wasn't complete.
Sa Portella, in English "the little gate", dates from the time of the Moors. The coat of arms, however, is that of the Aragonians. In the course of time, the name of the törchen was transferred to the entire district behind it.

The land in front of the fortress wall was artificially wrested from the sea. In the 60s and 70s, the Park de la Mar, the Park of the Sea, was created here. Parking spaces were originally planned here, due to the resistance of the population, among others. A. Miró was prevented from doing so.
The Palacio Real de La Almudaina was originally an Arab fortress. The 20,000 square meter palace is used by the Spanish king as his official residence, and parts of it are open to the public.

Unfortunately, it is very busy, precisely because it is well worth seeing. Very artistic design, beautiful glass windows. To be there in the morning when the light falls through the rose window above the altar into the cathedral.

On New Year's Eve 1229, Jaume I, King of Aragón, also known as the Conqueror, stormed the city of Palma after a three month siege.
As a sign of his victory, he laid the foundation stone of the Cathedral of St. Mary on the foundation walls of a mosque.

&lt;:)&gt; In truth, however, he was in a bit of a hurry to fulfill a promise that he had given the holy virgin. On his way across the sea his fleet got caught in a storm and he promised Mary that a church would be built if he survived the storm. “As a sign of my victory” sounds better than “I pissed my pants because of fear”. &lt;/ :)&gt;

The actual construction did not begin until around 70 years later under Jaume II, his son and lasted 300 years (until 1601). Over the centuries, the cathedral has been expanded again and again and only took on its present form around 100 years ago. Today it is only called "La Seu", Catalan for bishopric.

https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kathedrale_von_Palma

A footpath under the ramparts, which one can leave by awfully small stairs to climb the wall. Particularly recommended is the Museum of Modern Art, which is easily recognizable by the visible sculpture of stacked boxes.

faqs.question: How long does the City tour of Palma de Mallorca typically take?
faqs.answer: This city tour is approximately 5.8 kilometers long and generally takes around 1 hour and 32 minutes to complete at a relaxed pace, allowing plenty of time to explore the sights.

faqs.question: Is this city tour suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is classified as easy, making it perfect for beginners, families, and anyone looking for a leisurely exploration of Palma's urban landscape. It involves minimal elevation gain and mostly flat, pedestrian-friendly paths.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Palma de Mallorca city tour?
faqs.answer: The terrain primarily consists of cobblestone streets and well-maintained pedestrian pathways. You'll walk along grand boulevards, elegant waterfront promenades, and narrow, winding alleys in the Old Town. While largely flat, some areas around historical sites might have slight inclines.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: The tour takes you past many iconic landmarks. You'll see the Plaça Major (Main Square), the historic Almudaina Arch, and the ancient Arab Baths (Banys Àrabs). Other highlights include Sa Portella, the magnificent Royal Palace of La Almudaina, and the stunning Cathedral of Santa Maria of Palma (La Seu). Don't miss the View of La Seu Cathedral from Parc de la Mar.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to do this city tour?
faqs.answer: Palma de Mallorca is pleasant for city tours throughout much of the year. Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) offer comfortable temperatures and fewer crowds, making them ideal. Summer can be hot, so early mornings or late afternoons are recommended if visiting then.

faqs.question: Is the City tour of Palma de Mallorca dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, city walks in Palma are dog-friendly, especially on leash. However, access to specific historical sites, museums, or indoor attractions may be restricted for dogs. Always check individual venue policies if you plan to enter buildings.

faqs.question: Where can I park or access the start of the city tour?
faqs.answer: Palma de Mallorca has several public parking garages throughout the city center, such as those near Parc de la Mar or Plaça Major. The city is also well-served by public transport, including buses, making it easy to reach the starting points of urban walks without a car.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this city tour?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any permits to walk this city tour. The route primarily uses public streets and pathways. However, entrance fees may apply if you choose to visit specific attractions along the way, such as the Cathedral or the Royal Palace.

faqs.question: What should I wear or bring for a city tour in Palma de Mallorca?
faqs.answer: Comfortable walking shoes are essential, as you'll be on your feet for a few hours, often on cobblestones. Light, breathable clothing is recommended, especially during warmer months. Don't forget sunscreen, a hat, sunglasses, and a reusable water bottle to stay hydrated. A small backpack for essentials is also useful.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the city tour route?
faqs.answer: Yes, Palma de Mallorca's city center is full of cafes, restaurants, and bars. You'll find numerous opportunities to stop for a coffee, a traditional Mallorcan snack, or a full meal along the route, particularly in areas like the Old Town and near major plazas.

faqs.question: Does this city tour connect with other official trails?
faqs.answer:
Yes, this route incorporates sections of several official paths, including parts of

El Camí IT4TR4 El Camí Illenc: de l'Alguer a Formentera, Passeig de Dalt Murada, and Claustre. You'll also walk along smaller segments of la Portella and camí de l'Escollera.
